does: MechaRule proposes a pipeline for extracting symbolic rules from large language models by grounding them in neural circuits through sparse agonist activations. The method localizes neurons whose ablation disrupts rule-related behavior without hand-crafted hypotheses. The core contribution rests on two findings: first, sparse agonist effects exhibit an overtopping property where a few high-effect activations remain detectable within larger groups and dominate weaker ones, enabling efficient adaptive group testing with confidence-guided conservative pruning that requires only O(k log(N/k) + k) interventions to identify k agonists among N candidates. Second, agonists localize more reliably on data splits aligned with faithful rule behavior, with spectral clustering providing a rule-free fallback approach. The pipeline combines these insights to systematically identify which neurons implement specific decision rules in LLMs.
connects: This work builds on two intersecting research threads in interpretability. It extends mechanistic interpretability approaches that link model behavior to internal neural components through circuit analysis and ablation studies, moving beyond prior work that required hand-crafted hypotheses about which neurons matter. Simultaneously, it responds to limitations of symbolic rule extraction methods that learn ungrounded surrogates disconnected from actual model internals. The paper leverages recent advances in understanding sparse feature importance in neural networks and adapts group testing techniques from combinatorial search to efficiently identify key activations. It connects to work on model steering through neuron intervention and circuit discovery, while also engaging with the broader challenge of making LLM decision-making transparent through explainable AI frameworks that integrate symbolic reasoning with mechanistic accounts.
enables: This work enables several future directions in interpretable machine learning. By developing efficient methods to ground symbolic rules in neural circuits, it creates a foundation for extracting and verifying human-readable explanations that are actually implemented by models rather than post-hoc approximations. The O(k log(N/k) + k) sample complexity result suggests potential applications to model editing and control, where practitioners could selectively modify rule-implementing neurons to change model behavior. The approach could extend to understanding more complex decision rules beyond current scope, enabling better debugging of LLM failures and biases by pinpointing their neural mechanisms. Additionally, the spectral splitting fallback opens possibilities for discovering unexpected rules without prior knowledge of what rules to look for. This framework might facilitate safer model deployment by allowing auditors to verify that learned rules match intended behavior, and it could support transfer of interpretability insights across model architectures and domains.
